title: Gravitational waves refine cosmic expansion rate
oneliner: Combining gravitational wave signals with galaxy surveys yields a more precise measurement of how fast the universe is growing.
description: Scientists have used ripples in space-time from merging neutron stars, together with maps of millions of galaxies, to measure the Hubble constant. The new value is 67.9 km/s per megaparsec, with an uncertainty of only 6.4%. This method helps resolve a long-standing dispute between different cosmic measurements.
text:
Imagine trying to find out how fast a train is moving by timing how quickly it passes known landmarks along the track. Astronomers face a similar puzzle when measuring the [tag:expansion_of_the_universe]expansion of the universe[/tag]. They need reliable 'landmarks' whose true distances they know. One such landmark comes from the merger of two ultra-dense [tag:neutron_star]neutron stars[/tag]. These collisions shake the fabric of space-time, sending out ripples called [tag:gravitational_waves]gravitational waves[/tag]. Because the waves’ strength reveals the distance to the event, scientists call them 'standard sirens'—like a siren whose original volume is known, letting you figure out how far away it is.

In a new study, researchers combined the signal from the famous [tag:neutron_star]neutron star[/tag] merger GW170817 (and other such events) with a huge map of millions of [tag:galaxy]galaxies[/tag] from the Dark Energy Survey. The [tag:galaxy]galaxy[/tag] map provides additional information about how matter is distributed across the cosmos, including the mysterious [tag:dark_matter]dark matter[/tag] that was first compellingly evidenced by [scientist:Vera Rubin]Vera Rubin[/scientist]. By mixing these two approaches, they arrived at a new measurement of the [tag:expansion_of_the_universe]Hubble constant[/tag]—the rate at which the universe is stretching, named after [scientist:Edwin Hubble]Edwin Hubble[/scientist]. The result is 67.9 kilometers per second per megaparsec, meaning a [tag:galaxy]galaxy[/tag] 3.26 million light-years away would race away at about 68 km/s. The precision is an impressive 6.4%, far better than using [tag:gravitational_waves]gravitational waves[/tag] alone.

[callout]Gravitational waves from merging [tag:neutron_star]neutron stars[/tag] act as 'standard sirens'—cosmic beacons with a known intrinsic strength, allowing astronomers to calculate their distance.[/callout]

This finding is a significant step toward settling a heated debate. For years, two main methods have given conflicting answers: one based on the afterglow of the Big Bang suggests a slower [tag:expansion_of_the_universe]expansion[/tag], while measurements from exploding stars in nearby [tag:galaxy]galaxies[/tag] point to a faster one. The new hybrid technique falls in the middle and shows that combining independent methods is a powerful way to crack the mystery. The study also sharpened our estimate of how much matter (both visible and [tag:dark_matter]dark[/tag]) fills the universe.

[callout]The [tag:expansion_of_the_universe]Hubble constant[/tag] has been a source of debate, with measurements from the early universe (via the cosmic microwave background) and the late universe (via supernovae) disagreeing. This new method bridges the gap.[/callout]
fun_fact: The name 'standard siren' is a play on 'standard candle,' a term astronomers use for objects of known brightness, like certain supernovae.
mini: Two ultradense stars collided and sent tremors through space. By matching those shivers with a map of millions of galaxies, scientists have just clocked the universe’s growth spurt with record precision. The result: 67.9 km/s per megaparsec—hinting at a way to solve the biggest argument in cosmology.
